title: John's Testimony of Jesus
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: John’s Testimony of Jesus - Luke 3_15—18 Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The People's Expectation and Reasoning
subpoints: The people were in expectation — wondering if John might be the Christ
John the evangelist (not the Baptist) gives further detail in John 1:19-23
The people responded to John's ministry with thinking and questioning
A lack of response to preaching is evidence of the absence of the Spirit's work

outline.title: John's Self-Testimony: I Am Not the Christ
subpoints: John emphatically refuses any identification with the Messiah
He declares himself unworthy even to untie Jesus's sandals
A faithful minister exalts Christ rather than himself
John's ministry goal: 'I must decrease; he must increase'

outline.title: The Baptism of the Holy Spirit and Fire
subpoints: Holy Spirit and fire are synonymous — linked by a single 'with' in the Greek
This refers to the once-for-all Pentecostal baptism of the church
The Spirit continues His purifying work in the church like refining fire
The baptism of the Spirit relates to Christ's first coming and Pentecost

outline.title: The Winnowing Fan: Judgment and the Mixed Church
subpoints: John uses a harvest analogy — the farmer separates grain from chaff
The visible church is always a mixture of true believers and unbelievers
There are five types of people in every local church
Biblical prophecy often conflates first and second comings into a single vision

outline.title: The Urgency and Grace of Judgment
subpoints: Judgment begins at death — it is appointed for man once to die, then the judgment
Every day Christ delays His return is another day of grace for repentance
For believers, the judgment is nothing to fear — Christ was judged in our place
John preached both hard-stopping judgment and grace — good news accompanied warnings

summary:
In this morning sermon on Luke 3:15-18, Pastor Tito Lyro examines John the Baptist's testimony concerning Jesus as the people wonder whether John himself might be the Christ. The sermon explores the people's expectation as a model of hopeful, active faith, noting that their thoughtful engagement with John's preaching was itself evidence of the Spirit at work. John, by contrast, repeatedly denies being the Messiah and explicitly declares his unworthiness — pointing all attention to the coming One who is mightier than he.

The sermon gives careful attention to John's announcement that the Messiah would baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire, arguing from the Greek that 'Spirit' and 'fire' are joined by a single preposition and therefore describe the same reality. This was fulfilled at Pentecost and continues as the Spirit's purifying work in the church. Pastor Lyro then turns to John's harvest imagery in verse 17, using it to describe the mixed nature of the visible church — where five categories of people co-exist — and the separation that will occur at Christ's return.

The sermon concludes with a sobering treatment of the certainty and finality of judgment. Death brings immediate judgment; Christ's return brings the purification of the visible church and the resurrection of the righteous; and the final judgment follows. Yet for believers this is not terror but hope — Christ absorbed the condemnation we deserve. And every day He has not yet returned is another extension of God's patience and grace toward the repentant.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are studying the first year of Jesus' public ministry — about 19 sermons total, going morning and afternoon. We have seen so far that John the Baptist came to prepare the way for the long-expected Messiah. The last bona fide prophet had left the scene 400 years prior to this time in the ministry of Malachi. The heavens had been silent all the while — and yet many messianic figures were running around the desert claiming to be the promised Messiah. When you read about people's hesitancy to believe John and Jesus, do not take it in a vacuum. There were all kinds of people claiming to be the Messiah when John and Jesus came on the scene.

John the Baptist is different from them all. He dressed in camel skins. He ate locusts — which is just a really large grasshopper — and honey. And the biggest difference between him and all these other people claiming to be the Messiah is that John explicitly said: I am not the Messiah. I am here to prepare the way for the Messiah. I am here to point to him. He was a prophet, a preacher — the greatest of the Old Testament prophets, as Jesus says. His message was simple: repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.

This morning we consider John the Baptist's testimony concerning Jesus Christ. This afternoon we will look at John's baptism as he baptizes his cousin Jesus.

## The People's Expectation

Luke 3:15:

> *"Now as the people were in expectation, and all reasoned in their hearts about John, whether he was the Christ or not..."* (Luke 3:15)

John's ministry raised all kinds of questions in the minds of the people who heard him preach. Is he Elijah? Is he the prophet like Moses? Is he the Messiah? He did check all the boxes. The message sounded like what the Christ would be preaching. And John the Evangelist provides more details in John 1:19–23 about what was going through people's minds:

> *"Now this is the testimony of John, when the Jews sent priests and Levites from Jerusalem to ask him, 'Who are you?' He confessed, and did not deny, but confessed, 'I am not the Christ.' And they asked him, 'What then? Are you Elijah?' He said, 'I am not.' 'Are you the Prophet?' And he answered, 'No.' Then they said to him, 'Who are you, that we may give an answer to those who sent us? What do you say about yourself?' He said: 'I am the voice of one crying in the wilderness: Make straight the way of the Lord.'"* (John 1:19–23)

These questions are coming from a place of expectation — they were looking for something from the Lord. They could not wait to see what the Lord was going to do. This is the kind of expectation we should have as well — not in a demanding way, but in faith, knowing that all God's promises are yes and amen in Jesus Christ. We look for the evidence of grace God is displaying in the lives of those around us. We believe that God is at work. What good thing is God doing in my brother's life, in my sister's life?

Notice also that the people responded to John's ministry with thinking. They saw John, they heard John, and that led them to think. That is the greatest thing a preacher could hope for. A lack of response to the preaching of God's Word is evidence of the absence of the Spirit's work. And the Christian faith is never afraid of questions. J.C. Ryle: "The truth of Christ has nothing to fear from sober examination. We court inquiry."

## John's Testimony: I Am Not the Christ

Luke 3:16:

> *"John answered, saying to all, 'I indeed baptize you with water; but One mightier than I is coming, whose sandal strap I am not worthy to loose. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.'"* (Luke 3:16)

John does not want in any way to be confused with Christ. He says:

- Christ is more powerful than I am.
- Christ is worthy of more honor than I am.
- I am not even worthy of untying the laces of his muddy sandals.

Do not underestimate that last statement. This was a time of open-air sewage — there was no sewer system. People walked everywhere. To untie a master's sandals was the work of the lowest servant. And John says: I am not even worthy of that. He is a faithful minister: I am not he. He is greater than I. As Paul says in 2 Corinthians 4:5: "We do not preach ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord." J.C. Ryle: "A minister who is really doing his good will make us think more of Jesus every year we live." That is exactly what John is doing. When Jesus comes, John says: Behold, the Lamb of God. Do not look at me. I must decrease. He must increase.

## Baptism With the Holy Spirit and Fire

John says Christ's baptism differs from his own: "He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire" (Luke 3:16). In the original language, the Holy Spirit and fire are linked by one preposition — one "with" — making them synonymous. This is a reference to the coming of the Holy Spirit upon the church and the continuing purifying ministry of the Spirit in the life of the church.

How did this happen? The ascended Christ told his church in Acts 1:8: "You shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be witnesses to Me in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth." And at Pentecost, Acts 2:2–4:

> *"And suddenly there came a sound from heaven, as of a rushing mighty wind, and it filled the whole house where they were sitting. Then there appeared to them divided tongues, as of fire, and one sat upon each of them. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit."* (Acts 2:2–4)

That was the once-for-all baptism of the Holy Spirit upon the church, empowering her to accomplish all that her Head has set for her to accomplish. You do not need to be rebaptized with the Spirit. The church was baptized once, and that baptism continues to empower her. At the same time, the Holy Spirit continues to work in the church as a purifying agent — like fire purifying silver and gold — purifying us from all our imperfections until the day of the Lord Jesus Christ.

## Judgment: The Threshing Floor

This baptism relates to the first coming of Jesus. But as is often the case in prophetic literature, the first and second coming of the Son of God are seen as one event. John conflates the two comings in verses 16 and 17. More often than not, Scripture is more concerned with the fullness of the ministry of Christ than with when certain things will happen.

Verse 17:

> *"His winnowing fan is in His hand, and He will thoroughly clean out His threshing floor, and gather the wheat into His barn; but the chaff He will burn with unquenchable fire."* (Luke 3:17)

John uses a farm analogy. After the harvest of wheat is complete, the farmer separates the grain from the husk or chaff. He spreads the harvest on the threshing floor, then with a winnowing fork throws it into the air. The wind blows the chaff away — lightweight, good only for burning. The actual grain — heavier — falls back down. It is ready to be ground for food. The chaff is burned so hot it is difficult to put out.

Everyone in John's audience would have understood this. And it was a shocking message to the first-century Jew. They thought the Messiah was coming to judge the nations and exalt them. For John to say: no, the Messiah is coming primarily to judge you — that was a big shock.

John's proclamation of judgment teaches several things:

First, the harvest is currently taking place. The wheat is being brought in through the church, through conversions. People from every tribe, every tongue, every ethnic group are being added to the church. We need to pray that the Lord of the harvest would send laborers into the harvest.

Second, the visible church in its local representation is always a mix of believers and unbelievers. There is always grain and chaff in any gathering of the church of the Lord Jesus Christ. Only at his coming will there be a separation — as in the parable of the wheat and weeds in Matthew 13.

Third, this judgment is severe: "unquenchable fire." Jesus says in the parable of the sheep and the goats: "Depart from Me, you cursed, into the everlasting fire prepared for the devil and his angels." And elsewhere: "It is better to enter life with one foot than with two feet to be cast into hell into the fire that shall never be quenched."

## Conclusion: Grace Accompanies the Warning

Luke 3:18: "And with many other exhortations he preached to the people." The word "preached" here is the word for proclaiming good news. Though John proclaims a hard, stopping judgment, his preaching was also filled with grace. Grace accompanies every warning to flee from the judgment to come.

The Lord is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance (2 Peter 3:9). Every day he has not returned is another day of grace toward you so that you may repent of your sins. What do you believe concerning Jesus now? "The Spirit and the bride say, 'Come!' And let him who hears say, 'Come!' And let him who thirsts come. Whoever desires, let him take the water of life freely" (Revelation 22:17).

For those who believe: you do not have to fear the judgment, because Jesus Christ was judged for your sins. For he who knew no sin became guilty of sin for us, so that we could be made the righteousness of God in Him.
